text: Welcome to this video on optimization in machine learning.
Today we're going to talk about gradient descent, the backbone algorithm behind training neural networks.
At its core, gradient descent is an iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a loss function by moving in the direction of steepest descent.

The loss function measures how wrong your model's predictions are — it could be mean squared error for regression, cross-entropy for classification, or any differentiable function that captures prediction error.
The gradient of the loss function is a vector that points in the direction of greatest increase.
By moving in the opposite direction — the negative gradient — we nudge the model parameters toward values that reduce the loss.
We repeat this process thousands of times, each time computing the gradient and taking a small step downhill.
The intuition is like hiking down a mountain blindfolded: you feel the slope beneath your feet and take a step in the downhill direction.
Eventually, if the landscape is convex, you reach the valley — the global minimum.
If it's not convex, you might settle into a local minimum or saddle point, which is why initialization and learning rate matter so much.
The learning rate is the most important hyperparameter in gradient descent.
It controls how large a step we take in the direction of the negative gradient.
If the learning rate is too large, we overshoot the minimum — the loss bounces around or diverges entirely.
If it's too small, training takes forever and we might get stuck in suboptimal local minima.
A good learning rate typically falls between 0.0001 and 0.01, but the ideal value is problem-specific.

One way to find a good learning rate is the learning rate range test: train for a few epochs while increasing the learning rate exponentially, then pick the value just before the loss starts to increase.
Cosine annealing and step decay are popular learning rate schedules. Cyclical learning rates alternate between a lower and upper bound, which can help the model escape saddle points.

In practice, most practitioners use Adam or another adaptive optimizer instead of tuning the learning rate manually, but understanding what the learning rate does is fundamental to debugging training runs.
There are three main variants of gradient descent that differ in how much data they use to compute each gradient update.
Batch gradient descent computes the gradient over the entire training dataset before updating parameters.
This gives an accurate gradient estimate but is computationally expensive and slow for large datasets.
Stochastic gradient descent, or SGD, uses a single training example to estimate the gradient.
It's much faster per update but the gradient estimate is noisy because a single sample is a poor proxy for the full distribution.
Mini-batch gradient descent uses a small batch of samples — typically 32 to 256 — to estimate the gradient.
The batch size is large enough to get a reasonable gradient estimate and small enough to update frequently.
Small batches add noise that acts as a regularizer and can help escape sharp minima, while large batches train faster but sometimes converge to less generalizable minima.
Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes gradients efficiently in neural networks.
It is an application of the chain rule from calculus.
The chain rule says that if you have a composed function f of g of x, the derivative with respect to x is f prime of g of x times g prime of x.
In a neural network, the loss is a composition of many functions — one per layer — so we apply the chain rule repeatedly.
The forward pass computes the output of each layer and stores intermediate activations.
The backward pass then propagates the gradient from the output back to each layer using the stored activations to compute the local gradients.
This is efficient because each gradient only needs to be computed once and reused.
The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients become exponentially small as they propagate backward through many layers.
This was a major obstacle to training deep networks before ReLU activations and residual connections became standard.
Gradient clipping addresses the exploding gradient problem by capping gradient norms to prevent parameter updates from becoming dangerously large.
Adaptive optimizers like Adam, RMSprop, and AdaGrad modify the learning rate for each parameter individually based on gradient history.
Adam stands for Adaptive Moment Estimation and maintains two running averages: the first moment which is the mean of the gradients, and the second moment which is the uncentered variance.
The first moment acts like momentum, accumulating past gradients to smooth out the update direction.
The second moment scales down updates for parameters with large gradient variance, preventing overshooting.
The hyperparameters beta1 and beta2 control the exponential decay rates for these moments, with typical defaults of 0.9 and 0.999.
Adam almost always works well out of the box, which is why it is the default optimizer in most deep learning frameworks.
RMSprop is similar to Adam but uses only the second moment without the first, making it simpler but often slightly less effective.
Regularization techniques prevent overfitting, which happens when the model learns the training data too well and fails to generalize.
L2 regularization, also called weight decay, adds a penalty proportional to the sum of squared weights to the loss function.
This encourages the model to keep weights small, preventing any single feature from dominating.
The strength of regularization is controlled by a hyperparameter lambda — larger lambda means stronger regularization but risks underfitting.
L1 regularization adds a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute weights.
Unlike L2, L1 tends to produce sparse solutions where many weights become exactly zero, effectively performing feature selection.
Dropout is a different regularization approach: during training, a random fraction of neurons — typically 20 to 50 percent — are set to zero at each forward pass.
This forces the network to learn redundant representations and prevents co-adaptation of neurons.
At test time, all neurons are active but their outputs are scaled by the retention probability.
Dropout is particularly effective in fully connected layers and helped make deep networks practical before batch normalization became dominant.
Now let's talk about convolutional neural networks, or CNNs, which revolutionized computer vision and remain the dominant architecture for image understanding tasks.
A convolutional layer applies a set of learnable filters, also called kernels, to the input image or feature map.
Each filter slides across the input and computes a dot product at every position, producing a feature map that highlights where that pattern is detected.
Filters in early layers tend to detect low-level features like edges, corners, and color gradients.
Deeper filters combine those low-level patterns to detect textures, object parts, and eventually entire semantic concepts.
The stride controls how many pixels the filter moves between positions. A stride of 2 halves the spatial size of the output feature map.
Padding adds zeros around the border of the input so that filters can be applied at edge positions without reducing spatial dimensions.
Pooling layers downsample the feature maps to reduce spatial resolution and introduce a degree of translation invariance.
Max pooling selects the maximum activation within each pooling window, keeping the strongest detected feature and discarding its precise location.
Average pooling computes the mean activation within each window, preserving more spatial information but being less selective than max pooling.
The receptive field of a neuron is the region of the original input image that influences its activation.
In deep networks, neurons in later layers have large receptive fields spanning most of the image, letting them integrate global context when making predictions.
Residual networks, introduced by He et al. in 2015, solved the degradation problem — the observation that adding more layers beyond a certain depth made training accuracy worse, not better.
A residual block adds a skip connection that passes the input directly around the convolutional layers to the output of the block.
The block therefore learns a residual function — what to add to the identity — rather than learning the full transformation from scratch.
Skip connections also provide a direct gradient highway from the loss back to early layers, substantially relieving the vanishing gradient problem in very deep networks.
ResNet-50 and ResNet-152 were among the first networks to show that going hundreds of layers deep consistently improves accuracy when skip connections are used.
The transformer architecture, introduced in Attention Is All You Need in 2017, replaced recurrence and convolution with a mechanism called self-attention.
Self-attention allows every position in a sequence to directly attend to every other position in a single operation, regardless of how far apart they are.
In self-attention, each input token is projected into three vectors: a query, a key, and a value, each of lower dimension than the original embedding.

The attention score between two tokens is the dot product of one token's query with another token's key, divided by the square root of the key dimension to prevent the dot products from growing too large.
Dividing by the square root of the key dimension keeps the inputs to softmax in a range where gradients are well-behaved.
After applying softmax, the attention scores become a probability distribution over all positions in the sequence.
The output for each position is then a weighted sum of all value vectors, where the weights are the softmax attention scores.
Multi-head attention runs several independent attention operations in parallel, each using its own learned query, key, and value projections.
The outputs from all heads are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension with a final linear layer.
Using multiple heads lets the model simultaneously attend to different aspects of the input: one head might track subject-verb agreement while another tracks coreference.
Because attention is a set operation with no inherent notion of order, transformers require positional encodings to tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.
The original transformer added fixed sinusoidal functions of position to the input embeddings before the first layer.
Modern models often use learned positional embeddings or rotary position encodings, which encode relative distances and generalize better to longer sequences than seen during training.
Each transformer layer also contains a position-wise feed-forward sublayer: two linear transformations with a ReLU or GELU activation between them.
The feed-forward sublayer operates independently on each position and can be thought of as applying a small MLP to each token representation after attention has mixed information across positions.
Batch normalization is a technique introduced by Ioffe and Szegedy in 2015 that normalizes the activations within each mini-batch to have zero mean and unit variance.
After normalization, two learned parameters — scale and shift — allow the network to undo the normalization if that is beneficial for the task.
Batch normalization reduces internal covariate shift, the phenomenon where the distribution of each layer's inputs changes during training as the parameters of previous layers change.
By stabilizing activation distributions, batch normalization allows higher learning rates and reduces sensitivity to initialization.
Batch normalization also has a mild regularization effect because the mean and variance statistics computed over a mini-batch are noisy estimates of the true population statistics.
Layer normalization normalizes across the feature dimension of a single example rather than across examples in a batch.
It is preferred in transformers because it works correctly regardless of batch size and handles the variable-length sequences common in NLP without modification.
Early stopping monitors validation loss during training and halts training when the validation loss has not improved for a specified number of epochs, called the patience.
This prevents the model from memorizing training examples and ensures the selected checkpoint generalizes well, at the cost of not fully converging on the training set.
Learning rate warmup gradually increases the learning rate from near zero to its target value over the first several hundred or thousand optimization steps.

Warmup is especially important for transformer training because large early gradient updates, before the model has seen much data, can push parameters into poor regions of the loss landscape that are hard to escape.
Data augmentation applies random transformations — cropping, horizontal flipping, color jitter, cutout, mixup — to training images during each epoch.

Because the model sees a different version of each example on every pass through the data, augmentation effectively multiplies dataset size and is one of the cheapest forms of regularization available.
Transfer learning initializes a model with weights pretrained on a large dataset, typically ImageNet for vision or a large text corpus for language, and then fine-tunes on the target task.
The pretrained model has already learned general-purpose features — edges and textures for vision, grammar and word semantics for language — that transfer across many downstream tasks.
Fine-tuning typically freezes the early layers, which capture general features, and only updates the later layers and a task-specific head, which capture more task-specific patterns.

When the target dataset is very small, fine-tuning too many layers risks catastrophically overwriting the pretrained representations, so stronger regularization or a lower learning rate is applied to the pretrained layers.
